1: shadow O God, the nations have come into your inheritance; they have defiled your holy temple; they have laid Jerusalem in ruins. The defilement of the temple — Christ predicted precisely this: you see all these, do you not? Truly, I say to you, there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down (Matthew 24:2). The destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 is the judgment he foresaw, weeping over the city that would not receive him (Luke 19:41-44).
2: shadow They have given the bodies of your servants to the birds of the heavens for food, the flesh of your faithful to the beasts of the earth. The bodies given to birds — the imagery Revelation applies to the great eschatological battle: Come, gather for the great supper of God, to eat the flesh of kings... and the flesh of all men (Revelation 19:17-18). The judgment fallen on Jerusalem becomes the type of final judgment on all who oppose the Lamb.
3: shadow They have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them. Blood poured out around Jerusalem — the blood of the martyrs, and ultimately the blood of Christ shed in Jerusalem: it cannot be that a prophet should perish away from Jerusalem (Luke 13:33). His blood poured out is the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28) that gives meaning to all martyrs' blood.
4: shadow We have become a taunt to our neighbors, mocked and derided by those around us. Taunt and derision — the experience of Christ and his church. The nations mock the suffering people of God; Christ was mocked supremely (Matthew 27:39-44). Peter addresses this: if you are insulted for the name of Christ, you are blessed, because the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you (1 Peter 4:14).
5: shadow How long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? Will your jealousy burn like fire? The how long of the martyrs — the cry under the altar in Revelation: how long before you will judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth? (Revelation 6:10). Christ establishes the answer: will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night? (Luke 18:7). The how long is answered by the parousia.
6: shadow Pour out your anger on the nations that do not know you, and on the kingdoms that do not call upon your name! The pouring out of wrath on those who do not know God — Paul's language in 2 Thessalonians 1:8: inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. The judgment of the nations is now specifically tied to rejecting Christ and his gospel.
7: shadow For they have devoured Jacob and laid waste his habitation. The devouring of Jacob — Israel scattered. But Christ promises the ultimate gathering: he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other (Matthew 24:31). The scattered Jacob is gathered by the one who is the true Jacob, the Israel of God (Galatians 6:16).
8: shadow Do not remember against us our former iniquities; let your compassion come speedily to meet us, for we are brought very low. The plea for compassion to come speedily — the Incarnation itself. God's compassion coming to meet humanity brought very low is the Son of God born in a manger (Luke 2:7), taking on the lowliness of the human condition. He comes speedily to the one brought low; the parousia brings the final relief.
9: direct Help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name; deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name's sake! Atonement for God's name's sake — the motive Paul identifies in Romans 3:25-26: God presented Christ as a propitiation to show his righteousness and to demonstrate that he himself is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. The atonement vindicates God's glory and name while delivering sinners.
10: shadow Why should the nations say, Where is their God? Let the avenging of the outpoured blood of your servants be known among the nations before our eyes! Where is their God? — the taunt directed at the crucified Jesus: let God deliver him now, if he desires him (Matthew 27:43). The resurrection is God's public vindication — the avenging of the righteous one's blood made known among the nations through the apostolic proclamation (Acts 2:32-36).
11: shadow Let the groaning of the prisoner come before you; according to your great power, preserve those doomed to die! The groaning prisoner preserved from death — Christ in the tomb, the one doomed to die by the sentence of Pilate. God's great power preserves him not by preventing death but by raising him through it. All who are doomed to die in Adam are preserved in Christ (1 Corinthians 15:22 — in Christ shall all be made alive).
12: shadow Return sevenfold into the lap of our neighbors the taunts with which they have taunted you, O Lord! The sevenfold return of taunts — the divine vindication. Those who taunted God's people (and Christ) face the consequences of their mockery. Revelation 18:6 uses similar language of judgment on Babylon: pay her back double for her deeds. The taunts against the crucified one return on those who refuse repentance.
13: shadow But we your people, the sheep of your pasture, will give thanks to you forever; from generation to generation we will recount your praise. The sheep of your pasture — Christ's flock, who give thanks forever. The church's unending praise is grounded in the resurrection: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name (Hebrews 13:15). Generation after generation recounting God's praise is the church's mission until the parousia.
